explicit_text: I was born in New York City on May 12, 1922, and grew up attending several public schools there. The city was where I first got involved with music, starting with classical training and later moving into jazz. New York was also the place where I went to the High School of Music and Arts, which shaped much of my early musical development. It was a vibrant environment with many talented musicians around.

explicit_text: I attended Frederick Douglass Junior High School, also known as PS 139, during my early education in New York City. It was there that my English teacher suggested I apply to the High School of Music and Arts instead of Townsend Harris High School. This recommendation changed my path, leading me to focus more seriously on music. The school played a key role in directing me toward a musical career.

explicit_text: I was accepted into the High School of Music and Arts in New York City after my junior high teacher recommended it. It was a new school at the time, focused on musicians, and I initially wanted to play piano but ended up choosing bass violin because there were too many piano players. The school had many talented students, including Shorty Rogers and Brock Peters, and it was a place where many of us went on to have successful musical careers. It was a formative experience for me musically.

explicit_text: The High School of Music and Arts eventually became part of what is now LaGuardia High School, the performing arts high school in New York. When I attended, it was still called the High School of Music and Arts, but I understand LaGuardia is the current name. So, while I didn't attend LaGuardia per se, my school was its predecessor. It’s known today for its focus on the arts.

explicit_text: I originally wanted to go to Townsend Harris High School, which was considered an elite school in New York City. However, my English teacher told me I didn't have the brains for it and suggested I attend the new High School of Music and Arts instead. Townsend Harris was very academically demanding, and I took her advice, which ultimately led me to focus on music. So, while I never attended Townsend Harris, it was part of my early educational considerations.

His career path was quite different from mine but equally notable.

explicit_text: Margaret Murray was my first piano teacher when I was about four years old. She helped me develop my early skills and brought me along until I got pretty good. Her teaching laid the foundation for my classical training, which was very strict and disciplined. I owe a lot to her for starting me on this musical journey at such a young age.

explicit_text: Carl Diton was another of my piano teachers after Margaret Murray, and studying with him was a real struggle because he favored his star pupil, Calvin Jackson. Even though I wasn’t his favorite, Mr. Diton stuck with me and let me make mistakes while I learned. His teaching was strictly classical, and I learned quite a bit from him despite the challenges. He was an important figure in my classical music education.

explicit_text: Calvin Jackson was the star pupil of my piano teacher, Carl Diton, and he was older than me. Whenever I took lessons, Mr. Diton would compare my playing to Calvin’s, which was a bit tough but also motivating. Calvin was an excellent pianist, and I admired his talent even though I was still learning. We were part of the same classical music circle during my early training.

explicit_text: While I was at the High School of Music and Arts, Benny Goodman would come to the school with Teddy Wilson and Gene Krupa to play concerts for us. These events packed the gymnasium, and it was a big deal to see such jazz legends perform right at school. Benny Goodman was a major influence in my early exposure to jazz music, even though my training was classical at the time.

I thought he was the greatest pianist until I heard Art Tatum, which changed my perspective completely. Teddy Wilson’s playing was a big inspiration during my transition from classical music to jazz. Seeing him live at school was a memorable experience.

explicit_text: Art Tatum completely changed my view of piano playing when I heard him after being a fan of Teddy Wilson. I got to meet Art, and he was the one who helped me get my first job in music. His incredible talent was inspiring and pushed me to pursue jazz seriously. Meeting him was a pivotal moment in my career, and I owe a lot to his influence and support.

Although it was a humble beginning, it gave me valuable experience performing live and being part of show business. That summer on the road was an important step in my early career.

explicit_text: Romaine Brown was my cousin and a top violinist at the High School of Music and Arts, just like me. He was a fantastic musician but, like me, found it hard to make a living playing classical music. Eventually, he switched to piano and became more of an entertainer. Sadly, he passed away a few years ago, but he was well respected and played with a group called the New Friends of Music in New York.
